div.p: To all sons of holy Mother Church and to the faithful, present and future, who shall view or hear these letters, Duncan, earl of Carrick, sends greeting in the Lord. Know, all of you, that since the monks of Melrose were yearly bound to me in the payment of three merks, that is, two for the land of Beoch and one for the land of Auchnefure, just as it is set out in their charters: I have entirely released and perpetually quitclaimed the same three merks for the aforementioned monks in return for the forty merks sterling which the same monks have paid to me in my great need, that is, for my land to be put at liberty. For this reason, both I and my heirs and successors shall warrant this quitclaim of the aforesaid three merks against all men for the aforementioned monks for ever. And so that this quitclaim should be strong and enduring, I have placed as witness and guarantor the lord William, bishop of Glasgow, allowing that if at any time I or my heirs or successors should try anything against this or against the alms made to the same monks by me as set out in my charters, the same bishop and his successors shall compel us to observe all the foregoing at the request of the monks themselves, without knowledge of the cause, upon pain of ecclesiastical censure. As witness and defence of this matter I have applied my own seal to the present writing together with the seal of the lord William, bishop of Glasgow. With these witnesses: the lord Walter son of Alan, steward of Scotland, Ralph of Livingston, clerk, Alan my knight, Alan my son, parson of Kirchemanen and of Straiton, Master Hugh my clerk, and many others. Done in the year of the Lord 1236.
